title: Advanced Arduino Code
subtitle: Functions, Arrays & For-Loops — Write Cleaner Sketches!
overview: Students level up from basic Arduino blink sketches to writing clean, structured code using functions, arrays, and for-loops. By the end, they will refactor messy repetitive code into elegant, reusable sketches — the mark of a real programmer!

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — Arduino IDE screenshots, student code on screen, breadboard with LEDs, loop diagrams on whiteboard.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Screenshot of Arduino IDE with a function example
gallery.caption: Arduino IDE — where your code comes to life!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Whiteboard diagram showing a for-loop flowchart
gallery.caption: For-Loop — do it again, again, again... automatically!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Diagram showing a function box with input/output arrows
gallery.caption: Functions — a mini-program inside your program!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Photo of students typing code in Arduino IDE
gallery.caption: Students writing their first functions!

keyTopics: Why Clean Code? — Messy repeated code vs. elegant reusable code | DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself!
Functions — A named mini-program: void blinkLED() { ... } | Call it anywhere, anytime
Function Parameters — Passing values into a function: blinkLED(3, 500) → blink 3 times, 500ms delay
Return Values — Functions that give back a result: int addNumbers(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
Arrays — A numbered row of boxes: int pins[] = {3, 5, 6, 9, 11};
Accessing Array Elements — pins[0] is first, pins[4] is fifth | Index starts at ZERO!
For-Loop — Repeat with a counter: for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { ... }
For-Loop + Array = Power Combo — Loop through all LEDs with 3 lines instead of 20!
setup() vs loop() — setup() runs ONCE, loop() runs FOREVER | Know the difference!
pinMode() inside a for-loop — Set multiple pins as OUTPUT in one clean loop
Nested For-Loops — A loop inside a loop: rows × columns, wave effects
Debugging Tips — Serial.println() to print values; check index out of bounds errors

resources.title: Wokwi — Free Arduino Simulator (test code online!)

codeExamples.title: Messy Code — The Wrong Way (Copy-Paste Chaos!)
codeExamples.note: If you want to change the delay from 500 to 300, you must edit 10 lines! Very painful.

codeExamples.title: Clean Code — Using Arrays + For-Loop (The Right Way!)
codeExamples.note: Now changing delay is ONE edit. Adding a new LED is ONE line. This is clean code!

codeExamples.title: Functions with Parameters & Return Values
codeExamples.note: Functions with parameters are like recipes — you pass ingredients (inputs) and get a dish (output)!

codeExamples.title: Nested For-Loop — LED Wave Effect
codeExamples.note: Reverse loop uses i-- instead of i++. The condition flips too: i >= 0 instead of i < numLEDs.

activity.title: Refactor Challenge — Clean Up the Messy Sketch!
parts: Computer with Arduino IDE or Wokwi simulator
steps: Wire up 5 LEDs on breadboard to Arduino pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 11 (each with a 220Ω resistor)
Upload the MESSY code first — watch all 5 LEDs blink one by one
Discuss: what's wrong with the messy code? How many lines would we need for 10 LEDs?
Refactor Step 1: Create an array int ledPins[] = {3, 5, 6, 9, 11};
Refactor Step 2: Replace the setup() pinMode lines with a for-loop
Refactor Step 3: Write a blinkLED(pin, delayTime) function
Refactor Step 4: Replace the loop() body with a for-loop calling blinkLED()
Challenge 1: Change delay to 100ms — how many lines did you edit? (Just ONE!)
Challenge 2: Add a 6th LED — how many lines did you add? (Just TWO!)
Bonus Challenge: Make a forward-then-reverse wave effect using the nested loop pattern

quiz.q: What does DRY stand for in programming?
quiz.explanation: DRY = Don't Repeat Yourself! If you find yourself copy-pasting the same code, it's time to use a function or loop!

quiz.q: What is a FUNCTION in Arduino code?
options: A type of variable that stores numbers
A named block of reusable code that performs a specific task
The main power supply for the board
quiz.explanation: Function = Mini-program! You write it once, give it a name, then call it anywhere. void blinkLED() { ... } is a function.

quiz.q: What does this line do? → void blinkLED(int pin, int speed)
options: Defines a function named blinkLED that accepts two input values: pin number and speed
Creates an array of LED pins
quiz.explanation: This declares a function blinkLED with two parameters: pin (which LED) and speed (how fast). Parameters are the inputs to the function!

quiz.q: What is an ARRAY in programming?
options: A single variable storing one value
A list of values stored together under one name, accessed by index
quiz.explanation: Array = Row of boxes! int ledPins[] = {3, 5, 6, 9, 11}; stores 5 pin numbers. Access them as ledPins[0], ledPins[1], etc.

quiz.q: What index does the FIRST element of an array have?
options: It depends on the array size
quiz.explanation: Arrays start at index 0! So ledPins[0] is the first element, ledPins[1] is second. Always remember: start counting from ZERO!

quiz.q: What does this for-loop do? → for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
options: Runs the loop once then stops
quiz.explanation: Starts at i=0, runs while i < 5, increments i each time → runs for i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. That's 5 total runs!

quiz.q: In a for-loop: for (int i = 0; i < numLEDs; i++) — what does i++ mean?
options: i increases by 1 after each loop cycle
quiz.explanation: i++ means i = i + 1. After each loop cycle, i goes up by 1. This is how the loop steps through each element!

quiz.q: How do you access the 3rd LED in: int ledPins[] = {3, 5, 6, 9, 11};
quiz.explanation: Index starts at 0! ledPins[0]=3, ledPins[1]=5, ledPins[2]=6. So the 3rd LED (value 6) is at ledPins[2].

quiz.q: What is the difference between setup() and loop() in Arduino?
options: setup() is faster, loop() is slower
setup() runs once at start, loop() runs repeatedly forever
setup() controls LEDs, loop() controls motors
quiz.explanation: setup() runs ONCE when the Arduino powers on (great for pinMode). loop() runs FOREVER after setup — it's your main program cycle!

quiz.q: Which keyword is used to send a value BACK from a function?
quiz.explanation: return sends a value back from a function! Example: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } — calling add(3,4) gives back 7.

quiz.q: What data type is used for a function that returns NOTHING?
quiz.explanation: void means the function returns nothing — it just does a job. void blinkLED() { ... } runs commands but gives nothing back.

quiz.q: What will Serial.println(ledPins[1]) print if ledPins[] = {3, 5, 6, 9, 11}?
quiz.explanation: ledPins[1] is the SECOND element (index 1 = second position). ledPins[0]=3, ledPins[1]=5. So it prints 5!

options: A for-loop placed inside another for-loop
Two for-loops running at the same time
A for-loop inside a function only
quiz.explanation: Nested loop = loop inside a loop! Outer loop runs once, inner loop completes fully. Used for wave effects, grids, and patterns.

quiz.q: How do you set all 5 LED pins as OUTPUT using a for-loop and array?
options: for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT); }
quiz.explanation: Loop through each index from 0 to 4, use ledPins[i] to get the pin number, then call pinMode. Clean and scalable!

quiz.q: If you want to make a REVERSE LED wave (last LED first), which loop direction is correct?
quiz.explanation: Reverse loop: start at last index (numLEDs-1), go while i >= 0, decrement with i--. This steps backward through the array!
